attrs.alt: B2C Custom Policy — File Hierarchy and Execution Flow

content.text: What are Custom Policies?

Custom policies (Identity Experience Framework / IEF) are the advanced configuration layer of Azure AD B2C. While user flows provide a GUI-driven no-code experience, custom policies are XML files that give you complete control over every step of the authentication journey — what pages appear, what REST calls are made, what claims are transformed, and exactly what ends up in the token.

Use custom policies only when user flows cannot meet your requirements. Common reasons: step-up MFA based on resource sensitivity, migrating users from a legacy system with password validation against an existing API, A/B testing sign-in experiences, or chaining multiple IdPs with custom logic between them.
Custom Policy File Hierarchy

Custom policies follow a strict inheritance model. Each file inherits from its parent using the BasePolicyId element. The starter pack provides the base files; you extend them rather than modifying them directly.
File
Role
Should You Edit It?
TrustFrameworkBase.xml
Foundation — all claim types, display controls, base technical profiles
No — update starter pack version instead
TrustFrameworkLocalization.xml
Language localizations inheriting Base
No — use content definitions overrides
TrustFrameworkExtensions.xml
Your custom IdP configurations, technical profiles, claims transformations
Yes — primary customization file
SignUpOrSignIn.xml
Relying Party entry point — defines the UserJourney and output claims
Yes — define output claims and journey reference here
ProfileEdit.xml / PasswordReset.xml
Additional Relying Party files for other flows
Yes — one RP file per distinct user journey
Core IEF XML Concepts
ClaimsSchema — Declaring Claim Types

<!-- In TrustFrameworkExtensions.xml, add new claim types here -->
<BuildingBlocks>
  <ClaimsSchema>
    <ClaimType Id="loyaltyTier">
      <DisplayName>Loyalty Tier</DisplayName>
      <DataType>string</DataType>
      <UserHelpText>Your loyalty program tier</UserHelpText>
      <Restriction>
        <Enumeration Text="Bronze" Value="bronze" SelectByDefault="true"/>
        <Enumeration Text="Silver" Value="silver"/>
        <Enumeration Text="Gold" Value="gold"/>
      </Restriction>
    </ClaimType>
    <ClaimType Id="correlationId">
      <DisplayName>Correlation ID</DisplayName>
      <DataType>string</DataType>
    </ClaimType>
  </ClaimsSchema>
</BuildingBlocks>
TechnicalProfile — Calling a REST API

<!-- REST technical profile — calls external API to enrich claims -->
<TechnicalProfile Id="REST-EnrichClaims">
  <DisplayName>Enrich Claims from CRM</DisplayName>
  <Protocol Name="Proprietary"
             Handler="Web.TPEngine.Providers.RestfulProvider, Web.TPEngine, Version=1.0.0.0"/>
  <Metadata>
    <Item Key="ServiceUrl">https://api.contoso.com/b2c/enrich</Item>
    <Item Key="AuthenticationType">Bearer</Item>
    <Item Key="SendClaimsIn">Body</Item>
    <Item Key="AllowInsecureAuthInProduction">false</Item>
  </Metadata>
  <CryptographicKeys>
    <Key Id="BearerAuthenticationToken" StorageReferenceId="B2C_1A_RestApiBearerToken"/>
  </CryptographicKeys>
  <InputClaims>
    <InputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="objectId" PartnerClaimType="userId"/>
    <InputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="email" PartnerClaimType="emailAddress"/>
  </InputClaims>
  <OutputClaims>
    <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="loyaltyTier" PartnerClaimType="tier"/>
    <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="correlationId" PartnerClaimType="requestId"/>
  </OutputClaims>
  <UseTechnicalProfileForSessionManagement ReferenceId="SM-Noop"/>
</TechnicalProfile>
UserJourney — Orchestration Steps

<!-- UserJourney with 4 steps: sign-in page, validate, enrich claims, issue token -->
<UserJourney Id="SignUpOrSignInCustom">
  <OrchestrationSteps>

    <!-- Step 1: Show combined sign-in/sign-up page -->
    <OrchestrationStep Order="1" Type="CombinedSignInAndSignUp"
                       ContentDefinitionReferenceId="api.signuporsignin">
      <ClaimsProviderSelections>
        <ClaimsProviderSelection TargetClaimsExchangeId="FacebookExchange"/>
        <ClaimsProviderSelection ValidationClaimsExchangeId="LocalAccountSigninEmailExchange"/>
      </ClaimsProviderSelections>
      <ClaimsExchanges>
        <ClaimsExchange Id="LocalAccountSigninEmailExchange"
                        TechnicalProfileReferenceId="SelfAsserted-LocalAccountSignin-Email"/>
      </ClaimsExchanges>
    </OrchestrationStep>

    <!-- Step 2: Call REST to enrich claims -->
    <OrchestrationStep Order="2" Type="ClaimsExchange">
      <Preconditions>
        <!-- Skip if user signed in with Facebook (no need to enrich) -->
        <Precondition Type="ClaimsExist" ExecuteActionsIf="true">
          <Value>isFacebookUser</Value>
          <Action>SkipThisOrchestrationStep</Action>
        </Precondition>
      </Preconditions>
      <ClaimsExchanges>
        <ClaimsExchange Id="RESTEnrichClaims" TechnicalProfileReferenceId="REST-EnrichClaims"/>
      </ClaimsExchanges>
    </OrchestrationStep>

    <!-- Step 3: Issue the JWT -->
    <OrchestrationStep Order="3" Type="SendClaims"
                       CpimIssuerTechnicalProfileReferenceId="JwtIssuer"/>
  </OrchestrationSteps>
</UserJourney>
Uploading Custom Policies
Custom policies must be uploaded to the B2C tenant in dependency order: Base first, then Localization, then Extensions, then each Relying Party file. Uploading out of order causes validation errors.
Upload via PowerShell

Connect-MgGraph -TenantId 'contoso.onmicrosoft.com' -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.TrustFramework'

function Upload-B2CPolicy {
    param([string]$FilePath)
    $policyId  = [System.IO.Path]::GetFileNameWithoutExtension($FilePath)
    $policyXml = Get-Content $FilePath -Raw -Encoding UTF8
    $bytes     = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes($policyXml)
    Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PUT `
        -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/trustFramework/policies/$policyId/`$value" `
        -Body $bytes `
        -ContentType 'application/xml'
    Write-Host "Uploaded: $policyId"
}

# Upload in dependency order
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/TrustFrameworkBase.xml'
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/TrustFrameworkLocalization.xml'
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/TrustFrameworkExtensions.xml'
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/SignUpOrSignIn.xml'
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/ProfileEdit.xml'
Upload-B2CPolicy './policies/PasswordReset.xml'
CI/CD Pipeline Upload (GitHub Actions)

# .github/workflows/deploy-b2c-policies.yml
name: Deploy B2C Custom Policies
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['b2c-policies/**']

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Get access token (client credentials)
        id: token
        run: |
          TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST \
            'https://login.microsoftonline.com/${{ secrets.B2C_TENANT_ID }}/oauth2/v2.0/token' \
            -d 'grant_type=client_credentials' \
            -d 'client_id=${{ secrets.B2C_CLIENT_ID }}' \
            -d 'client_secret=${{ secrets.B2C_CLIENT_SECRET }}' \
            -d 'scope=https://graph.microsoft.com/.default' \
            | jq -r '.access_token')
          echo "access_token=$TOKEN" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Upload policies in order
        env:
          TOKEN: ${{ steps.token.outputs.access_token }}
        run: |
          for policy in TrustFrameworkBase TrustFrameworkLocalization TrustFrameworkExtensions SignUpOrSignIn ProfileEdit PasswordReset; do
            curl -s -X PUT \
              "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/trustFramework/policies/${policy}/\$value" \
              -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
              -H 'Content-Type: application/xml' \
              --data-binary @"b2c-policies/${policy}.xml"
            echo "Uploaded: $policy"
          done
Managing Signing Keys for Custom Policies

Custom policies reference cryptographic key sets (B2C_1A_*) for token signing, SAML certificates, and REST API bearer tokens. Keys are created in the B2C tenant and referenced in TrustFrameworkExtensions.xml by their key set ID.

Connect-MgGraph -TenantId 'contoso.onmicrosoft.com' -Scopes 'TrustFrameworkKeySet.ReadWrite.All'

# Create the token signing key set (if not already present from starter pack)
New-MgTrustFrameworkKeySet -Id 'B2C_1A_TokenSigningKeyContainer'
New-MgTrustFrameworkKeySetKey `
    -TrustFrameworkKeySetId 'B2C_1A_TokenSigningKeyContainer' `
    -BodyParameter @{ use = 'sig'; kty = 'RSA' }
Write-Host 'Token signing key created.'

# Store a REST API bearer token as a secret key
New-MgTrustFrameworkKeySet -Id 'B2C_1A_RestApiBearerToken'
New-MgTrustFrameworkKeySetSecret `
    -TrustFrameworkKeySetId 'B2C_1A_RestApiBearerToken' `
    -BodyParameter @{
        use = 'enc'
        k   = '<base64url-encoded-secret-value>'
    }
Write-Host 'REST API bearer token stored in key set.'
Debugging Custom Policies
Enable Application Insights logging by adding InstrumentationKey to the TrustFrameworkExtensions metadata.
Use the VS Code extension 'Azure AD B2C' to syntax-check policies before uploading.
Test policies from the portal using 'Run now' on the Relying Party file — errors appear inline.
AADB2C90219: typically a missing key set — create the B2C_1A_* key set referenced in Extensions.
AADB2C90045: policy validation error — check the XML against the starter pack schema and look for typos in TechnicalProfileReferenceId.
Application Insights query: filter on b2clogin.com events and search for the correlationId that appears in the browser error.
Custom Policies vs User Flows — Decision Guide
Requirement
User Flow
Custom Policy
Basic sign-up / sign-in with social IdPs
Yes
Overkill
Profile editing with standard attributes
Yes
Overkill
Validate user email against external allow-list
Via API connector
Via REST technical profile
Step-up MFA based on claimed resource
No
Yes — Preconditions on step
Migrate users from legacy system with password validation
No
Yes — REST validate + MigrateUser flag
A/B testing sign-in page variants
No
Yes — Preconditions route to different ContentDefinitions
Custom SAML token issuance (SP-initiated)
No
Yes — SAML Issuer technical profile
Chained IdP federation with custom claim logic between steps
No
Yes — multiple ClaimsExchange steps
Claims Transformations

Claims transformations are reusable functions that manipulate claim values — concatenating strings, comparing values, creating boolean flags, or mapping enum values. They are declared in BuildingBlocks and referenced inside TechnicalProfiles.
Common Transformation Examples

<BuildingBlocks>
  <ClaimsTransformations>

    <!-- Concatenate givenName and surname into displayName -->
    <ClaimsTransformation Id="CreateDisplayName" TransformationMethod="FormatStringMultipleClaims">
      <InputClaims>
        <InputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="givenName" TransformationClaimType="inputClaim1"/>
        <InputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="surname"   TransformationClaimType="inputClaim2"/>
      </InputClaims>
      <InputParameters>
        <InputParameter Id="stringFormat" DataType="string" Value="{0} {1}"/>
      </InputParameters>
      <OutputClaims>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="displayName" TransformationClaimType="outputClaim"/>
      </OutputClaims>
    </ClaimsTransformation>

    <!-- Set a boolean flag if loyaltyTier equals 'gold' -->
    <ClaimsTransformation Id="IsGoldTier" TransformationMethod="CompareClaimToValue">
      <InputClaims>
        <InputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="loyaltyTier" TransformationClaimType="inputClaim1"/>
      </InputClaims>
      <InputParameters>
        <InputParameter Id="compareTo"  DataType="string" Value="gold"/>
        <InputParameter Id="operator"   DataType="string" Value="equal"/>
        <InputParameter Id="ignoreCase" DataType="string" Value="true"/>
      </InputParameters>
      <OutputClaims>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="isGoldMember" TransformationClaimType="outputClaim"/>
      </OutputClaims>
    </ClaimsTransformation>

  </ClaimsTransformations>
</BuildingBlocks>
Using a Transformation Inside a TechnicalProfile

<TechnicalProfile Id="LocalAccountSignUpWithLogonEmail">
  <!-- ... other config ... -->
  <OutputClaimsTransformations>
    <!-- Run CreateDisplayName after collecting givenName and surname -->
    <OutputClaimsTransformation ReferenceId="CreateDisplayName"/>
    <!-- Then check if the new user is a gold-tier member -->
    <OutputClaimsTransformation ReferenceId="IsGoldTier"/>
  </OutputClaimsTransformations>
</TechnicalProfile>
ContentDefinitions — Custom HTML Templates

ContentDefinitions map B2C page types (sign-in, sign-up, MFA, error) to HTML template URLs. This is how you replace the default Microsoft-hosted pages with pages hosted on your own CDN, while B2C injects its JavaScript API to wire up the form fields.

<!-- In TrustFrameworkExtensions.xml BuildingBlocks section -->
<ContentDefinitions>

  <!-- Override the unified sign-in/sign-up page -->
  <ContentDefinition Id="api.signuporsignin">
    <!-- Must be served over HTTPS; CORS must allow b2clogin.com -->
    <LoadUri>https://cdn.contoso.com/b2c/unified.html</LoadUri>
    <RecoveryUri>~/common/default_page_error.html</RecoveryUri>
    <DataUri>urn:com:microsoft:aad:b2c:elements:contract:unifiedssp:2.1.5</DataUri>
    <Metadata>
      <Item Key="DisplayName">Signin and Signup</Item>
    </Metadata>
  </ContentDefinition>

  <!-- Override the MFA page -->
  <ContentDefinition Id="api.phonefactor">
    <LoadUri>https://cdn.contoso.com/b2c/mfa.html</LoadUri>
    <RecoveryUri>~/common/default_page_error.html</RecoveryUri>
    <DataUri>urn:com:microsoft:aad:b2c:elements:contract:multifactor:1.2.5</DataUri>
  </ContentDefinition>

</ContentDefinitions>
CORS requirement: your HTML hosting server must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://<tenant>.b2clogin.com. B2C's page contract JavaScript will not load without it.
Relying Party File — Output Claims

The Relying Party file (e.g. SignUpOrSignIn.xml) is the entry point for a specific user journey. It defines which UserJourney to run and exactly which claims appear in the resulting token. Claims not listed in the RP OutputClaims are never included, regardless of what the journey collected.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<TrustFrameworkPolicy
  xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
  xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"
  xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/online/cpim/schemas/2013/06"
  PolicySchemaVersion="0.3.0.0"
  TenantId="contoso.onmicrosoft.com"
  PolicyId="B2C_1A_SignUpOrSignIn"
  PublicPolicyUri="http://contoso.onmicrosoft.com/B2C_1A_SignUpOrSignIn">

  <BasePolicy>
    <TenantId>contoso.onmicrosoft.com</TenantId>
    <PolicyId>B2C_1A_TrustFrameworkExtensions</PolicyId>
  </BasePolicy>

  <RelyingParty>
    <DefaultUserJourney ReferenceId="SignUpOrSignInCustom"/>
    <TechnicalProfile Id="PolicyProfile">
      <DisplayName>PolicyProfile</DisplayName>
      <Protocol Name="OpenIdConnect"/>
      <OutputClaims>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="displayName"/>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="givenName"/>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="surname"/>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="email"/>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="loyaltyTier"/>
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="isGoldMember"/>
        <!-- objectId becomes the sub claim in the JWT -->
        <OutputClaim ClaimTypeReferenceId="objectId" PartnerClaimType="sub"/>
      </OutputClaims>
      <SubjectNamingInfo ClaimType="sub"/>
    </TechnicalProfile>
  </RelyingParty>

</TrustFrameworkPolicy>
Listing and Downloading Uploaded Policies

Connect-MgGraph -TenantId 'contoso.onmicrosoft.com' -Scopes 'Policy.Read.All'

# List all uploaded IEF policies
$policies = Get-MgTrustFrameworkPolicy
foreach ($p in $policies) {
    Write-Host $p.Id
}

# Download the content of a specific policy
$policyId = 'B2C_1A_SignUpOrSignIn'
$content  = Get-MgTrustFrameworkPolicyContent -TrustFrameworkPolicyId $policyId
# $content is a byte array — convert to string
$xml = [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString($content)
$xml | Out-File "./policies/$policyId.xml" -Encoding UTF8
Write-Host "Downloaded: $policyId.xml"
KQL: Custom Policy Execution Errors

// Find B2C sign-in failures related to custom policy errors — last 24h
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ResultType != 0
| where AppDisplayName != ''
| extend ErrorCode = tostring(Status.errorCode)
| extend FailureReason = tostring(Status.failureReason)
| extend PolicyId = tostring(AdditionalDetails[0].value)
| where PolicyId startswith 'B2C_1A'
| summarize Count = count() by ErrorCode, FailureReason, PolicyId
| order by Count desc
Summary
Custom policies (IEF) are XML files that define authentication journeys at a granular level — claim types, technical profiles, orchestration steps, and relying party output.
The file hierarchy is: TrustFrameworkBase → TrustFrameworkLocalization → TrustFrameworkExtensions → Relying Party files. Always extend, never modify the base.
TechnicalProfiles define how to interact with IdPs, REST APIs, and UI pages; UserJourneys sequence them into orchestration steps with optional Preconditions.
Policies are uploaded via the Graph API (/trustFramework/policies) in dependency order — base before extensions before relying party.
Signing keys and secrets used by technical profiles are stored in key sets (B2C_1A_*) and referenced by key container ID in the policy XML.
Use user flows for standard scenarios and custom policies only when you need logic that user flows cannot express.
